entries.form_section: Syr. regularly gen_b , esp. in the compound (al gen_b ; see s.v. ˁl gb prep. The form gwnb) at PsJ Dt 14:9 is influenced by the JBA word meaning "tail" as shown by incorrect variants of dwnb) at TgJob 40:17. JBAmb also gmb) and even gwp) .
entries.end_notes: In Syriac the meaning is always "side" with the exception of the usage referring to the hump of a camel. In Babylonian dialects, however, the reference seems to be to the upper quadrant of an animate being viewed from the side; i.e., as with "sides," animate creatures have two of them!

entries.end_notes:
For a possible example see DNWSI s.v. gbyḥy.
In spite of the uncertainties expressed by Schulthess, the word seems well established (see references in DJPA). The root is surely gbn where the final nun has been elided in colloquial western Aramaic and a final laryngeal substituted, perhaps under the influence of gbḥ "bald". Thus Levy's interpretation as a strengthened form from gbyh "gross gewachsen" is to be rejected.

It is hardly probable that the meaning "eunuch" in both LJLA and the Syriac lexical tradition is due to a inner Jewish Aramaic corruption from גוזי. The semantic development from "inner" is unobjectionable (i.e. member of the royal inner circle); the origin of gwz, gwzy in this meaning is more troubling. See also note s.v. srys n.m.

entries.form_section: JLA pl. gissiyN; gisasiyN ; Mand. pl. gisania;
entries.end_notes:
Gal. also gyc ; though the distribution suggests the possiblity that this spelling refers there to a physical side as opposed to a direction.
The example from the Syriac bowl with the fem. pl. is so far unique; though undoubtedly due to Jewish Aramaic influence. From Akkadian (Assyrian dialect) giššu < gilšu "rear flank." See AIOA 52 and n. 102.
See also s.v. gs, the same word but with the doubled /s/ properly preserved. The long vowel here is probably an error.
The great confusion undoubtedly at least partially arises from the homonymy with the borrowing from Greek of γεῖσον, "cornice" or "parapet," for a physical segment on top of a wall.

entries.form_section: Female camel may also be explicitly gmlh; rarely gwml) ? Syr. pl. of "beam" gm:lwt), gm:lt), gm:l)
entries.end_notes:
For the double gender see Bh Gr2 11(13ff.)
See also s.v. gml "camel driver."
In the NT proverbial saying (Matthew 19:24, Mark 10:25, and Luke 18:25) about a camel and the eye of a needle, the native Syriac lexical tradition seems to be convinced that our lemma is to be interpreted as a large rope used on ships. See the statement of BarBahlul (500:19): ܚܒܠܐ ܥܒܝܐ ܕܐܣܪܝܢ ܒܗ ܣܦܝ̄ܢܬܐ.
Colloquial Arabic forms of جملون “gable” demand the previous Aramaic existence of a diminutive of “roof beam”.

entries.end_notes: This word, a hapax in the entire corpus, has traditionally been rendered “dart”. Note the variant לונכיין, i.e. from Greek λόγχη. This usage of the MT שְׁבָטִים is equally uncertain. A possible key to the interpretation of all of the forms, though, may lie in the JS text cited s.v. šbṭ #4 and the note there, i.e., all forms (MT, Targum, and Syriac) should be rendered “thorny branch” or the like, which would explain the need for Joab to use three of them to do away with Absalom. If correct, one needs to compare Akkadian giṣṣu A a type of “thorn”; note especially the text cited CAD G p. 99a “a thorn in your heart."

A culture word of uncertain origin. See Ciancaglini, ILS, 145.
The usage at Hatra may be influenced by inner-Arabic semantic developments. Akkadian gurābu is only found in late texts and is a loanword into that language, as noted by CAD G.
LS2, as is his unfortunate wont, renders the Judith passage as "cistern" or the like, following the Greek. But that is not what the Syriac says.

entries.end_notes: NB: This is the only example in JLA out of 134 examples where BH אַמָּה is not rendered by its cognate.  Clearly the intent is to indicate that this is not a simple physical measurement of something else but the actual size of his arm from wrist to elbow.  On the other hand, there are 8 examples in LJLA (all in Targum Pseudo-Jonathan) where the Hebrew measurement is rendered this way without obvious reason.  In the latter part of the text, however, it only occurs with "thousands".

entries.primary_gloss: to learn or know by heart
entries.end_notes: Both grs#1 and grs#2 are almost certainly from the same original lemma, the etymon of various nouns for "crushed grain." Here the metaphoric meaning of "to go through something in a very finely detailed manner" has prevailed. In grs#2 the literal meaning "to crush" has been generalized (in the D stem) as "to destroy" and then an intranstive formed by back-formation as the G in Syriac.

This is a difficult form of dubious meaning. Obviously the targum is concerned to render MT גנות in a way that expresses a pagan context, but whether the text is correct or what the precise meaning of the term would be escapes us. Nor do variants that split it into two words (see Levy make much sense.
That it occurs only in the Isaiah targum suggests a nonce invention as a play on "garden," but note the similar meanings of such forms as gnyn n.m.#2 and various homographs of simple gny.

entries.end_notes: The reading in the digitized British Library manuscript differs from the published versions of the line which use a grammatically sanitized text. We have corrected the text provided us from the Clarke/Aufrecht edition accordingly. The form itself, however, may be an error in any case. If correct it must be taken as a substantivized p.p. from the verb gnn #2 "to construct a canopy" and refer to the frequent concern in the laws of purity to the difference between contamination from the air in an enclosed space and an open one. Nor can the reading כגנונא, i.e., "like a canopy", be excluded. We assume that is what the scribal correction intended.
